steps.prompt: Which word is the main finite verb?

hints.venire: 'Venire' is an infinitive meaning 'to come' — it depends on 'Sinite'
hints.pueros: 'Pueros' is a noun meaning 'children' in the accusative
hints.me: 'Me' is a pronoun meaning 'me' in the accusative

hints.present indicative: Present indicative 2nd pl. would be 'sinitis' — 'Sinite' lacks the -s
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'sinatis' — 'Sinite' is a direct command
hints.infinitive: Infinitive would be 'sinere' — 'Sinite' is the plural imperative

hints.sisto, sistere, stiti, statum: These are the parts of 'sistere' (to halt/place)
hints.sido, sidere, sidi, —: These are the parts of 'sidere' (to sit down/settle)
hints.signo, signare, signavi, signatum: These are the parts of 'signare' (to mark/sign)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'pueri' — the -os ending marks accusative plural
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'pueris' — 'pueros' is accusative
hints.dative: Dative plural would be 'pueris' — 'pueros' is the accusative direct object

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'pueri' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'pueri' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'pueri' ends in -i

hints.imperative: Imperative would be 'veni' (sg.) or 'venite' (pl.) — 'venire' ends in -re
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'veniat' — 'venire' is infinitive
hints.gerund: The gerund would be 'veniendi/veniendum' — 'venire' is the infinitive form

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'ego' — 'me' after 'ad' is accusative
hints.ablative: 'Me' can be ablative, but after 'ad' it is accusative
hints.dative: Dative would be 'mihi' — 'me' after the preposition 'ad' is accusative

translation: Allow the children to come to me
